steps: A system of two linear equations has infinitely many solutions only when both equations represent the exact same line.
The given line is \(y = \frac{2}{7}x + 3\). For \(y = mx + b\) to be the same line, we need \(m = \frac{2}{7}\) and \(b = 3\). The question asks for \(b\).

Test all four choices for \(b\):
  • Choice A — '-3': \(b = -3\) gives \(y = \frac{2}{7}x - 3\). Same slope, different intercept — parallel lines, no solution. ✗
  • Choice B — '\(-\frac{1}{3}\)': \(b = -\frac{1}{3}\) gives a different intercept from 3. Parallel lines, not identical. ✗
  • Choice C — '\(\frac{1}{3}\)': \(b = \frac{1}{3}\) gives a different intercept from 3. Parallel lines, not identical. ✗
  • Choice D — '3': \(b = 3\) matches the given equation exactly, making \(y = \frac{2}{7}x + 3\) identical to the first equation. Every point on this line satisfies both equations simultaneously. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
